original_hypothesis.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS in Core Functions due to Unbounded Token Array
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `configuredTokenContracts` array grows unboundedly with each new token configuration. Functions like `setLockDuration` and `getLockedWeightedValue` iterate over this entire array, leading to a permanent DoS if the array becomes too large.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If the admin configures many tokens over the protocol's lifecycle (even if they are later marked inactive), core functionalities will eventually exceed the block gas limit and break permanently.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures a large number of tokens over time
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deployment scripts show multiple tokens being configured, but no mechanism exists to remove them from the array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` iterating over unbounded array

preconditions: Admin has configured a large number of tokens (e.g., > 100)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded iteration over a state array that only grows
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Once the array reaches a critical size

operational_questions: How many tokens are expected to be supported?
Is there a plan to deprecate and remove old tokens?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call `setLockDuration` and verify it reverts due to out-of-gas

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin routinely adds new tokens for various promotional events or lockdrops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The gas cost of iterating the array exceeds the block limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users can no longer change their lock duration, and external contracts can no longer calculate weighted values

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent breakage of core protocol features
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade as there is no function to remove tokens from the array
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Lock duration updates and reward distribution

validation_result.impact_summary: The configuredTokenContracts array grows without bound and has no removal mechanism. Multiple user-facing and view functions iterate over the entire array, creating a path to permanent gas exhaustion DoS if the array grows large enough.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a real code defect. The `configureToken` function appends to the `configuredTokenContracts` array whenever a new token is configured, but provides no mechanism to remove entries. Three functions iterate over this full array: `setLockDuration` (external, user-facing), `getLocked` (view), and `getLockedWeightedValue` (view, used by external contracts for reward calculations). Each iteration performs multiple storage reads (SLOADs), which are gas-expensive. While practical exploitation requires the admin to configure a very large number of tokens (100+), the code contains no safeguard: no maximum array length check in `configureToken`, no token removal function, and no alternative data structure that avoids full iteration. This is a design-level code defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at LockManager.sol line 239 is confirmed. The `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` loop at that location iterates over the unbounded `configuredTokenContracts` array. The `configureToken` function at lines ~121-131 only pushes to the array and never pops. No removal function exists anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No maximum length check on configuredTokenContracts. Array only grows, never shrinks. No removal function exists in the contract.
Iterates over the entire unbounded configuredTokenContracts array. Each iteration performs multiple cold SLOADs (~2600 gas each). With a sufficiently large array, this exceeds the block gas limit.
Also iterates the full unbounded array. As a view function, this impacts external contracts that call it (likely AccountManager for reward calculations), causing those transactions to fail as well.
Iterates full array and allocates a memory array of the same size. Both iteration and memory allocation costs grow linearly with array size.

calling_sequence: 1. Admin calls configureToken() for each new token -> configuredTokenContracts.push(_tokenContract) at line 127
2. Array grows with each unique token configuration, never shrinks
3. User calls setLockDuration() -> iterates configuredTokenContracts at line 239
4. External contract calls getLockedWeightedValue() -> iterates configuredTokenContracts at line 376
5. External contract calls getLocked() -> iterates configuredTokenContracts at line 346
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The configureToken function (line 127) performs `configuredTokenContracts.push(_tokenContract)` without any length check. There is no function in the entire contract that removes entries from this array. The setLockDuration function (line 239) then iterates `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` performing at minimum 1 SLOAD for the address from the array (~2100 gas warm, 2600 cold) plus 1 SLOAD for lockedTokens quantity (~2600 cold) per iteration. At 500+ tokens, the gas cost for setLockDuration alone exceeds 3-4M gas, and at thousands of tokens it can exceed block gas limits. getLockedWeightedValue performs even more SLOADs per iteration (quantity, active flag, decimals, usdPrice).

deployment_assumptions_checked: Admin configures a large number of tokens over time
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The configure-token-test.ts script configures only 3 tokens (USDB, WETH, ETH). This shows initial deployment uses few tokens. The scripts do not reveal any plans for bulk token addition, nor do they show any maximum token count constraint being enforced at the deployment level. No mechanism for token removal is deployed.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid but requires administrative cooperation (intentional or through operational accumulation). The admin must configure a large number of tokens over the protocol's lifecycle. Each `configureToken` call with a new token address permanently adds to the array. Even marking tokens as inactive does not remove them from the iteration. The threshold for DoS depends on the chain's block gas limit (Blast L2 typically 30M gas). Rough estimate: ~2000-3000 tokens could cause setLockDuration to exceed 30M gas. For getLockedWeightedValue which does more work per iteration, the threshold may be lower (~1000-1500 tokens). While this number seems unrealistically high for normal operations, the code provides zero safeguards against it.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the array grows sufficiently large: (1) setLockDuration becomes permanently uncallable, preventing users from updating their lock durations. (2) getLockedWeightedValue becomes uncallable, potentially breaking external reward distribution contracts. (3) getLocked becomes uncallable, breaking UI/frontend integrations. Recovery requires contract upgrade since there is no removal function. Severity is HIGH for the code defect itself, though practical exploitation likelihood is LOW-MEDIUM given the admin-only nature of token configuration.
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum array length check in configureToken: require(configuredTokenContracts.length < MAX_CONFIGURED_TOKENS)
Add a function to remove/deactivate tokens from the configuredTokenContracts array (swap-and-pop pattern)
Consider refactoring setLockDuration to not iterate over all tokens - instead, store per-player active token lists
Consider pagination for getLocked and getLockedWeightedValue view functions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with mock dependencies. 2. Call configureToken for N unique token addresses (e.g., N=500, 1000, 2000). 3. Lock a small amount for a test user on one token. 4. Measure gas cost of setLockDuration for increasing N values. 5. Identify the N at which gas exceeds block gas limit. 6. Repeat for getLockedWeightedValue.
uncertainties: Exact block gas limit on Blast L2 (assumed ~30M based on typical L2 configs)
Whether admin would realistically configure enough tokens to trigger the issue
Warm vs cold SLOAD costs depend on whether slots were accessed in the same transaction
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of setLockDuration and getLockedWeightedValue at various configuredTokenContracts.length values
Blast L2 block gas limit confirmation
Review of any off-chain governance processes that might limit token configuration frequency
